EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought 7 to 14 inches of snow to the mountains of southeast Utah. Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph created blowing and drifting of snow.

The storm track favored northeast Utah with respect to snowfall and increased snowpack, while cold season precipitation and the mountain snowpack remained well below normal across southeast Utah. Little change was noted across eastern Utah, with abnormally dry conditions persisting across southeast and east central Utah.  Although most of northeast Utah was no longer listed as having any abnormal dryness or drought, the water supply remained a concern following years of below normal precipitation and drought. For a continuation on this drought situation please see the February 2006 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Occasional storms brought some snowfall to extreme eastern Utah, but widespread precipitation capable of impacting the dryness across the region did not occur.  Mountain snowpack remained exceptionally low across southeast Utah while the mountains of northeast Utah continued to experience near average snowpack.  In short, there was little change in the dryness and water supply concerns for eastern Utah, with abnormally dry conditions continuing for the entire area except for the Eastern Uinta Mountains. For a continuation on this drought situation please see the March 2006 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although mountain snowpack was very close to 100 percent of normal for the mountains of northeast Utah, abnormally dry conditions continued to plague the remainder of far eastern Utah.  In stark contrast, mountain snowpack across southeast Utah was generally at 50 to 60 percent of normal, resulting in abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions in the Four Corners area.  In addition to snowpack deficiencies, water supply continued to be a concern for the entire area as the impact from long term drought over previous years continued to be felt. For a continuation on this drought situation please see the April 2006 Storm Data publication.
A moist Pacific low pressure system produced 3 to 6 inches of snowfall across the lower elevations of extreme southeast Utah.
A fast moving but potent storm brough as much as a foot and a half of snow to central and northern Utah in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds resulted in widespread power outages in Blanding as large trees up to 60 feet tall and tree limbs fell onto power lines.  A total of 7 lines were downed and 4 power poles were broken. The majority of the power outages lasted about three and one-half hours.  Several homes sustained wind damage, mostly to roofs. Shingles were blown off homes and some metal roofs were blown off or curled up in some places. Trampolines were tossed around yards and neighborhoods, several small sheds were blown over and caused damage to equipment, adjacent homes and vehicles. In one instance a shed was wrapped around a power pole which broke the meter for a nearby trailer. Some portable garages were toppled and caused damage to vehicles. Downed power lines sparked two fires.

This incident was named the Westwater 2 Fire and occurred about 7 miles northeast of Cisco. About 2,800 acres of pinyon pine, juniper, and grass were consumed by the blaze, and protection was needed for several structures. The cost of fighting the fire was estimated at 22,000 dollars.

This incident was named the Pot Hole Fire and occurred about 25 miles northeast of Vernal. About 1,398 acres of sagebrush, grass, and timber were consumed by the blaze. Several residences were threatened by the fire.

This fire was named the Willow Creek Wildfire and occurred in a remote area on the Tavaputs Plateau approximately 20 miles north of Thompson Springs. The fire burned over 200 acres which consisted of chapparral, juniper, and timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding at and around Arches National Park and the nearby DOE Atlas tailings cleanup site, including Highway 191 and State Road 128. The entrance road and parking lot to Arches National Park visitors center were covered with 2 to 3 feet of water in places, resulting in water damage to vehicles.  Water flowed under doorways into some buildings, and a 2 foot levee behind the visitor center was breeched. Runoff formed a river which went through a new maintenance building.  Numerous mudslides also occurred near the entrance to the park resulting in closure of the park road for a period of time. Bloody Mary Wash near the park entrance flooded across Highway 191 causing traffic to backup to Moab.  There was significant undercutting near the pavements edge along a two mile stretch of Highway 191.  Highway 191 and State Road 128 in the area were shut down for a period of time due to flood waters and debris on the road.  The storm dumped 1.59 inches of rain in 20 minutes at the Arches National Park visitors center,  and a total of 1.69 inches within one hour.  Up to 4 inches of rain fell within one hour at the Atlas tailings cleanup site which is monitored by the DOE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved slowly across southern California and Arizona and then moved east northeast across southeast Colorado. Deep moisture was transported across eastern Utah ahead of this system and continued to wrap around the low pressure center as it moved east of the area. The moist and unstable atmosphere along with a strong upper level jet resulted in moderate to heavy snow across the area with strong winds over some of the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 12 inches occurred with amounts of 12 inches over the majority of the area. Widespread wind gusts from 20 to 50 mph created blowing and drifting snow. A wind gust of 69 mph was recorded on the La Sal Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 14 inches occurred with wind gusts of 20 to 33 mph that created blowing and drifting snow. Most of the snow fell in a 14 hour period from 2300 MST on the 27th to 1300 MST on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy Snow fell in the Wasatch and Uinta Mountains of Northern Utah...with significant accumulations reaching down to the bench areas of the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sandy received 8 inches of snow in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought significant snowfall to the I-15 Corridor from Fillmore to Cedar City as well as the adjacent mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of 12-15 inches total snowfall, including 10 inches in 6 hours at Cedar City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved slowly across southern California and Arizona and then moved east northeast across southeast Colorado. Deep moisture was transported across eastern Utah ahead of this system and continued to wrap around the low pressure center as it moved east of the area. The moist and unstable atmosphere along with a strong upper level jet resulted in moderate to heavy snow across the area with strong winds over some of the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches occurred with 3 inches over 50 percent of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold storm system brought arctic air and heavy snow to much of the state over a 3 day period. The cold front pushed through northern Utah and stalled across central Utah  on the evening of the 27th.. Resulting in heavy snow across central Utah and the mountains of the north. A secondary shot of very cold air moved over the Great Salt Lake late in the day on the 28th...producing lake-effect snowfall south and southeast of the lake which lasted into the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In 12 hours, Ben Lomond peak and Snowbasin received 14 inches of snow. Snowbasin also received 28 inches of snow for the storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist Pacific Storms brought snow and strong winds to mainly northern Utah. Over 1 foot of snow and wind gusts in the 60-70 mph range were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts reported across the northwest deserts of Utah included 73 mph at Gunnison Island, 66 mph at Wendover, 61 mph at Rosebud RAWS, and 58 mph at the Salt Flats Dugway sensor 23 miles northeast of Ibapah.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt. Baldy gusted to 103 mph and 70 to 80 mph gusts were recorded across higher peaks of the Cottonwoods. Fifteen inches of snow fell at Alta Collins and 13 inches fell at Snowbird.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure accompanied by a strong cold front and deep Pacific moisture brought widespread snow to eastern Utah. Heavy snow and strong winds buffeted the mountains and high plateaus. Moderate to heavy snow blanketed lower elevations of east central Utah and portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches blanketed much of the area, including Canyonlands National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged drought over recent years continued to have lingering effects across southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of significant precipitation in October caused the dryness and drought conditions to abate to a degree across southeast Utah. By the end of the month only extreme southeast Utah was categorized as being under abnormally dry conditions in the U.S. Drought Monitor.|For a continuation on this drought situation please see the November 2006 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow ahead of a strong storm system over the desert southwest transported very moist air across the Four Corners area that resulted in heavy rains across east central and southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding around Moab and Arches National Park. A park ranger reported water two and one-half feet deep in places. Court House Wash and the Yellow Cat area were the hardest hit. Trails and roads in the park became impassable and had to be closed. Campgrounds were flooded and campers and hikers had to be evacuated. Reports from Grand County law enforcement indicated 6 to 12 inches of water running across Highway 191 on the north edge of Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system and associated cold front roared across the eastern Great Basin and produced significant snowfall and strong winds across the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 12 inches occurred and wind gusts of 25 to 50 mph caused blowing and drifting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow ahead of an upper level trough brought areas of heavy rainfall to east central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed from heavy rains in the south end of Moab. Fast moving water up to 2 feet deep flooded roads, including Highway 128 near the ALCO Store. Roads were closed until road graders could remove mud and rocks. The road crossing Onion Creek was also closed due to high water and debris. At least 10 houses had water damage and flooded basements, including a pre-school. One-half inch of rain fell at the fire station between 1100 and 1130 MST, and rainfall was much heavier to the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure accompanied by a strong cold front and deep Pacific moisture brought widespread snow to eastern Utah. Heavy snow and strong winds buffeted the mountains and high plateaus. Moderate to heavy snow blanketed lower elevations of east central Utah and portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall averaging 12 inches occurred and wind gusts of 20 to 37 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure accompanied by a strong cold front and deep Pacific moisture brought widespread snow to eastern Utah. Heavy snow and strong winds buffeted the mountains and high plateaus. Moderate to heavy snow blanketed lower elevations of east central Utah and portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall averaging 12 inches occurred and wind gusts of 35 to 48 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure accompanied by a strong cold front and deep Pacific moisture brought widespread snow to eastern Utah. Heavy snow and strong winds buffeted the mountains and high plateaus. Moderate to heavy snow blanketed lower elevations of east central Utah and portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 17 inches occurred. Winds gusted 29 to 48 mph, and locally to 87 mph at La Sal Peak. The strong winds caused blowing and drifting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure accompanied by a strong cold front and deep Pacific moisture brought widespread snow to eastern Utah. Heavy snow and strong winds buffeted the mountains and high plateaus. Moderate to heavy snow blanketed lower elevations of east central Utah and portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 6 inches blanketed the Interstate 70 corridor from Green River to the Colorado state line. Snowfall amounts diminished to the south with 3 to 4 inches in the Arches National Park and Moab areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow ahead of an upper level trough brought widespread rainfall and areas of heavy rain to east central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused Mill Canyon to run wall-to-wall with fast moving water which surged into Upper Courthouse Wash. Flash flooding also occurred along Old Dead Horse Point Road about 20 miles west northwest of Moab, when water about 2 feet deep quickly filled a normally dry wash. A vehicle became stuck while trying to cross a road which had already received some flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged drought over recent years continued to have lingering effects across southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of significant precipitation in October caused the dryness and drought conditions to abate to a degree across southeast Utah. By the end of the month only the Abajo Mountains were categorized as being under abnormally dry conditions in the U.S. Drought Monitor.|For a continuation on this drought situation please see the November 2006 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold autumn airmass settled over eastern Utah behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated sensors and spotter reports indicated minimum temperatures of 30 to 31 degrees across the majority of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought significant early season snowfall to the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early season snowfall of 4 to 6 inches was measured above the 8000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms over portions of east central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from 1/2 inch to 1 inch in diameter was covering Interstate 70 around Thompson Springs. The hail was up to 5 inches deep in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist south flow produced persistent rain over east central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washes ran bankfull throughout Arches National Park with numerous reports of flooding and mud and debris flows. The main flooding occurred along Salt Wash and the road to Delicate Arch was washed out. A number of campers and recreationists were evacuated from the park and a few hikers and campers became trapped for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copius amounts of subtropical moisture streamed across the Four Corners area in a southerly flow ahead of a low pressure system over the southwestern states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rainfall resulted in widespread flooding and flash flooding across much of east central and southeast Utah, including San Juan County and Canyonlands National Park. In Canyonlands National Park many roads and trails were closed due to heavy rains and flooding. Around Needles Outpost, Highway 211 among other roads and trails were inundated with mud and water that made them impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copius amounts of subtropical moisture streamed across the Four Corners area in a southerly flow ahead of a low pressure system over the southwestern states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rainfall resulted in widespread flooding and flash flooding across much of east central Utah, including Arches National Park. One park ranger said it was the worst flooding she had seen in Arches in 14 years. In the Park, Courthouse Wash ran 12 to 14 feet deep. Roads were closed and washed out in some areas, while some trails were obliterated. The trailhead of Delicate Arch was impassable as flood waters from Salt Wash created a 50 to 60 foot wide channel, blocking pedestrian access. On the evening of Friday, October 6, park employees and Grand County Search And Rescue volunteers ferried 20 adults and 4 children to safety after raging waters in Salt Wash blocked their return from Delicate Arch near the trailhead. Yellow Cat Canyon was hit hard with flooding and it was reported that areas outside the park were a mess. Flood waters did not recede in some areas of the park until Sunday afternoon, October 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system and cold front swept across the eastern Great Basin and brought heavy snow and strong winds to the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This early season storm produced 6 to 12 inches of snow and winds of 20 to 30 mph. Highway 191 was closed for a period of time from the Highway 45 intersection to Steinaker Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass behind a strong cold front brought freezing temperatures to the lower valleys of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 27 to 32 degrees.
